original_abstract: This paper investigates the problem of distributed consensus control for multi-agent systems characterized by general linear dynamics over directed communication topologies. Conventional consensus protocols often rely on the smallest non-zero eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian—a global parameter that limits the scalability and autonomy of the system. To overcome this limitation, we propose a distributed adaptive consensus protocol that requires only local agent dynamics and relative state measurements from neighbors. We demonstrate that this protocol ensures leader-follower consensus for any communication graph possessing a directed spanning tree rooted at the leader. Because the proposed control law is independent of global communication graph metrics, it is entirely distributed in nature. Furthermore, we extend our framework to accommodate more complex scenarios involving multiple leaders.
humanized_abstract: Distributed consensus for multi-agent systems with general linear dynamic models and directed communication topologies This paper deals with the distributed consensus control problem for multi-agent systems with general linear dynamic models and directed communication topologies. Most consensus algorithms depend on the smallest eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian, which is a global parameter. An adaptive distributed consensus protocol that only requires the knowledge of the local dynamic model of each agent and the relative state measurements of its neighbors is then proposed. We show that leader-follower consensus is reached for any communication graph with a directed spanning tree rooted at the leader. As the control law is designed to be independent of the global parameters of the communication graph, it is fully distributed. Finally, the proposed strategy can be easily extended to the case of multi-leaders.

Most consensus algorithms depend on the smallest eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian, which is a global parameter. An adaptive distributed consensus protocol that only requires the knowledge of the local dynamic model of each agent and the relative state measurements of its neighbors is then proposed. We show that leader-follower consensus is reached for any communication graph with a directed spanning tree rooted at the leader. As the control law is designed to be independent of the global parameters of the communication graph, it is fully distributed. Finally, the proposed strategy can be easily extended to the case of multi-leaders.
